narrative:
The market sits in Business As Usual territory this morning, with a composite stress score of 28 out of 100 keeping conditions in a cautious but not alarming posture. What anchors the regime here is the absence of systemic credit distress and a VIX that, while elevated, has not broken into territory associated with genuine dislocation; the recent equity softness reads more as a routine pullback than a structural deterioration.

VIX at 21.5 sits at the 66th percentile of its ten-year history, meaning volatility is modestly above the long-run median but well short of panic thresholds, and the contango term structure signals that options markets are not pricing in an imminent shock. Credit is firmly confirming the benign read: HY spreads at 280 basis points sit at only the 16th percentile of their five-year range, and IG at 78 basis points tells a similar story, with both z-scores near negative one, indicating spreads are actually tighter than average. The bond market is, in short, not buying the equity weakness.

Emerging markets are the clearest area of stress worth flagging, with six of eight EM markets in the red over the past five days. EEM has shed 5.8 percent and Brazil via EWZ is down 5.3 percent, suggesting dollar strength or country-specific political risk is weighing disproportionately on the developing world rather than reflecting a broad global risk-off move. The technology sector via XLK is also down 5.6 percent on the week, a notable underperformance that warrants monitoring given its index weight, while energy and healthcare have held up as relative havens.
